channel.title: Whamcloud Community JIRA
channel.description: This file is an XML representation of an issue

item.title: [LU-18446] Ensure CPUs are configured with the proper power management mode for optimal latency
item.description:
Recently running fio tests with certain processors has been showing more than 50% performance degradation compared with previous processor generation.

Doing more investigations has proven that the problem was caused by the latency to exit from idle state when some deeper sleeping/saving power-management modes were configured.

In recent (6.8+) kernels, there are already several drivers preventing this behaviour by using the PM-QoS Linux kernel framework and ensuring to "bump" the power-management mode to guarantee a minimal latency during on-going IOs/operations, for the CPUs/Cores that will be involved to handle completions.

We have done some similar instrumentations in Lustre-client code and have been able to recover the expected performance.
I am presently porting these changes to master and will push a patch asap.
item.summary: Ensure CPUs are configured with the proper power management mode for optimal latency

status.description: A resolution has been taken, and it is awaiting verification by reporter. From here issues are either reopened, or are closed.

comments.comment:
"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/59285/
Subject: LU-18446 ptlrpc: fix cpu_latency_work() completion time
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: 69bf4ec09bed9ac8cd47d5f101d8de2ff4886d32

"Andreas Dilger <adilger@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/59285
Subject: LU-18446 ptlrpc: fix cpu_latency_work() completion time
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 63efdfe1ba1c22e4e7ab1c06f3e42d415f6b63a9

I was looking at a debug log, and I see a flood of messages related to this patch. It looks like this happens when the time has reached exactly the deadline (but not after, which is what is checked), so it prints this message for a jiffie until the timer changes because the timer is rescheduled for 0 jiffies:

00000100:00000040:0.0:1747485134.433532:0:2211:0:(connection.c:87:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba9000 (cpu 0) has not reached its deadline 4303604531, at 4303604531
00000100:00000040:0.0:1747485134.433534:0:2211:0:(connection.c:87:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba9000 (cpu 0) has not reached its deadline 4303604531, at 4303604531
00000100:00000040:2.0:1747485134.433535:0:15638:0:(connection.c:87:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba91c0 (cpu 0) has not reached its deadline 4303604531, at 4303604531
00000100:00000040:2.0:1747485134.433537:0:15638:0:(connection.c:87:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba91c0 (cpu 0) has not reached its deadline 4303604531, at 4303604531
00000100:00000040:0.0:1747485134.433537:0:2211:0:(connection.c:87:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba9000 (cpu 0) has not reached its deadline 4303604531, at 4303604531
00000100:00000040:2.0:1747485134.433539:0:15638:0:(connection.c:87:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba91c0 (cpu 0) has not reached its deadline 4303604531, at 4303604531
00000100:00000040:0.0:1747485134.433539:0:2211:0:(connection.c:87:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba9000 (cpu 0) has not reached its deadline 4303604531, at 4303604531
00000100:00000040:2.0:1747485134.433542:0:15638:0:(connection.c:87:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba91c0 (cpu 0) has not reached its deadline 4303604531, at 4303604531
:
:
00000100:00000040:3.0:1747485134.369102:0:622:0:(connection.c:75:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba92a0 (cpu 0) has reached its deadline 4303604466, at 4303604467
00000100:00000040:2.0:1747485134.369102:0:15638:0:(connection.c:75:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba91c0 (cpu 0) has reached its deadline 4303604466, at 4303604467
00000100:00000040:1.0:1747485134.369102:0:29461:0:(connection.c:75:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba90e0 (cpu 0) has reached its deadline 4303604466, at 4303604467
00000100:00000040:0.0:1747485134.369103:0:2211:0:(connection.c:75:cpu_latency_work()) work item of ffff88008aba9000 (cpu 0) has reached its deadline 4303604466, at 4303604467

I'm thinking that the condition in cpu_latency_work() should be changed to consider the work done at the timer instead of after, which answers the question of whether the "else" case would ever happen:

-        if (time_after64(jiffies_64, latency_qos->deadline)) {
+        if (time_after_eq64(jiffies_64, latency_qos->deadline)) {
                 :
         } else {
                /* XXX Is this expected to happen?
                 * anyway, reschedule for the remaining time
                 */

Just in case, and since I had to do some in-house testing on some Arm platforms, patch do not introduce any regression there, performance even increased a very little bit ..

I did not have time to verify current Arm power-management feature integration with PM-QoS nor how it can be tweaked already, so there may be some more testing to be done and why not some possible future improvements in this area that may lead to more performance increase at some point? And by the way, these capabilities may also depend of the Arm CPU model/profile...

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/57039/
Subject: LU-18446 ptlrpc: lower CPUs latency during client I/O
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: 54a64ea818d936cb52c263fd34bb359972ae9fb1

Thanks, Bruno - Very helpful.

sihara , fyi

> Can you give more details on your fio?  Maybe just the command line or file if that's all right?

Yes, for sure Patrick !
The command line looks like :

for i in 1 2 4 8 16 32 64 128 192 256 320 ; do TEST=randread NJOBS=${i} fio ./fio_Lustre.job ; done

with a fio_Lustre.job file containing :
 

#FIO job file
[global]
name=lustre-generic
directory=/lustre/fsw/admin/bfaccini/default
filename_format=fio.$jobnum.$filenum
unique_filename=0
filesize=64G
ioengine=psync
bs=1M
direct=1
#direct=0
#iodepth=64
time_based=1
runtime=60s
ramp_time=30s
group_reporting=1
disable_lat=1
disable_clat=1
disable_slat=1
rw=randread
allow_file_create=1
 
[socket0]
numa_cpu_nodes=0
numa_mem_policy=bind:0
numjobs=${NJOBS}
 
[socket1]
numa_cpu_nodes=1
numa_mem_policy=bind:1
numjobs=${NJOBS}

Bruno,

Can you give more details on your fio?  Maybe just the command line or file if that's all right?

Here is a table summarising the single node fio (randread) performance :

NJOBS Target perf Original perf perf with patch
1 2.5 1.05 2.56
2 5.24 2.14 5.26
4 10.8 4.36 10.5
8 21.3 8.68 20.9
16 40 16.9 40
32 65.4 32.2 64.1
64 84 56.8 83.4
128 90.8 79.6 89.9
192 91.7 85.2 91.5
256 91.9 87.4 91.8
320 91.8 89.7 91.9

"Bruno Faccini <bfaccini@nvidia.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/57068
Subject: LU-18446 ptlrpc: add PM-QoS configure checks
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 023c2161eca46e3559a74cfbc8a7da0f714d71b1

wrongly created due to wrong push...

Hello Andreas, and thank you already for your comments!

Will try to answer your questions and also better detail some aspects of my current patch.

> Or is this limited to cores that are actively processing IO and the power mode will go back to normal once the IO is complete?
Yes, this applies only to CPUs/Cores selected to handle RPCs/replies from the target server.

> Is this in the context of the application thread, or the ptlrpcd thread?

ptlrpcd.

> The danger is if IO takes any length of time (seconds is not unreasonable for bulk IO on a busy server) then the CPU cores may continually be kept in the high power state.
Patch presently uses the known max wait time for the target.

> Definitely there needs to be a tunable parameter that allows enabling/disabling this functionality so that it is possible to trade off power/heat vs. performance.
I can add this for sure.

Current patch is using an array to keep each required struct to interface with PM-QoS and maintain each CPU/Core state, but I have also investigated a way to use a per-cpu area for this if you think it is preferable ?

"Bruno Faccini <bfaccini@nvidia.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/57039
Subject: LU-18446 ptlrpc: lower CPUs latency during client I/O
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: fb8218804428a1c72989714daf95bbe506c7fbe3

This is interesting, but is it Lustre's job to keep the CPU hot? I'm not a big fan of competing products that are just busy-waiting on (dedicated) CPU cores just because they can't do anything better...

Or is this limited to cores that are actively processing IO and the power mode will go back to normal once the IO is complete? Is this in the context of the application thread, or the ptlrpcd thread?

The danger is if IO takes any length of time (seconds is not unreasonable for bulk IO on a busy server) then the CPU cores may continually be kept in the high power state.

Definitely there needs to be a tunable parameter that allows enabling/disabling this functionality so that it is possible to trade off power/heat vs. performance.
